blurb: Cheerful, long-blooming prairie native with drooping yellow (sometimes mahogany-banded) petals around a tall sombrero-like cone — easy, drought-tough, and worked hard by bees all summer.
seasons: Yellow/red sombrero flowers early summer–fall; ferny gray-green foliage; dark seed cones into winter
wildlife: Bees and butterflies; seeds for birds

shots.cap: Summer — flowering drift · © Paul Johnson (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — rated to about USDA zone 4, so Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters pose no problem for this drought-tough prairie native.[2]
care.planting: Plant container stock in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall; it establishes fast and often blooms its first or second year. In containers use a gritty, free-draining mix and don't overwater — it sulks in soggy soil. Once sited it self-sows to renew itself.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun. It thrives in the open, blazing exposure of high-altitude prairie.[1][2]
care.soil: Average, dry to medium, well-drained soil; tolerant of shallow rocky and calcareous ground but intolerant of moist, heavy clay. On Boulder's clay, plant high and amend for drainage rather than richness.[2]
care.water: Low; highly drought-tolerant once established. Little to no supplemental water needed in our semi-arid climate after the first season.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space roughly 12–18 inches apart to allow the clumps room; it reseeds to fill gaps.[2]
care.propagation: Very easy from seed and the practical method — it has a taproot and is best not divided. Sow in spring or fall; seedlings typically won't flower until their second year. Let cones dry to brown on the plant to allow self-seeding or seed collection.[1][2]
care.sow: Direct-sow in spring or fall; germination is easy. Plants flower the second year from seed.[1][2]
care.stratify: A cold-moist period helps — about 9 weeks at ~40°F improves germination of stored seed.[1]
care.bloom: Long bloom from early summer into fall — roughly June into September, and later if moisture allows — in yellow (and mahogany) drooping rays around a tall central cone.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low to medium; deadhead to prolong bloom or leave cones to dry for self-seeding and birds. No serious pests or diseases.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Reseeds readily where soil is open; let the cones turn dry and brown before cutting back so seed can mature and drop.[1]
care.troubles: Few problems; rots only in wet heavy clay, and is otherwise pest- and disease-free.[2]

references.name: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Ratibida columnifera
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Ratibida columnifera

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental pollinator plant; documented only for a traditional leaf tea, not as a modern food.[1]
edible.caution: Only a traditional beverage tea from the leaves is documented — that ethnobotanical/medicinal use is not the same as established food safety, so this guide treats it as inedible. It is not notably poisonous, but don't rely on it as food.[1]
